narrative: As a white male applicant with a full-time study plan, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting the likelihood of passing on the first try based on academic and background factors. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.20 (well below the average of 3.21), LSAT score of 33 (lower than the typical 36.7), Year 3 school decile ranking of 2 (versus an average of 5.53), and low family income bracket—and predicted a 48.3% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This elevated risk threshold led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar to maintain their strong pass rates. The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 decile ranking, which signals weaker performance relative to your peers in that critical later stage of schooling. Counterbalancing this somewhat was your LSAT score, which nudged the prediction slightly toward passing, though not enough to offset other concerns given how it trails the norm. Your low family income also worked in your favor, pulling the odds a bit toward success, perhaps reflecting resilience or other strengths associated with that background in the data. Compared to typical students, your academic deciles in both Year 1 and Year 3 are in the lower range, which amplified the model's caution despite your full-time commitment aligning with the vast majority (over 92%) of successful applicants. While these top factors dominated the decision, your overall profile didn't demonstrate the consistent high performance the model links to bar passage. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a modest background—many in similar situations have succeeded with targeted improvements. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or tutoring, and gathering letters highlighting your resilience from low-income circumstances. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could shift your predicted odds significantly, opening doors at this or other programs. You're capable of turning this around with focused effort.
